title: Sam Altman: Bits and Bytes: Sam Altman is latest tech executive to join local government - The Economic Times
text:
Bits and Bytes: Sam Altman is latest tech executive to join local government SECTIONS Bits and Bytes: Sam Altman is latest tech executive to join local government ETtech Last Updated: Nov 24, 2024, 06:00:00 AM IST Rate Story Follow us Share Font Size Abc Small Abc Medium Abc Large Save Print Comment Synopsis Sam Altman joins San Francisco’s mayor-elect Daniel Lurie’s team to address the city’s public safety crisis. Meanwhile, Meta faces German lawsuits over a massive 2021 data leak, Australia targets under-16 social media bans with hefty fines, and Google’s Chrome browser may be sold for $20 billion amid US antitrust measures, potentially reshaping search and AI industries. Agencies Sam Altman has joined a growing list of tech leaders taking up roles in San Francisco’s government, after mayor-elect Daniel Lurie named the OpenAI CEO one of seven co-chairs of his transition team.
Assembly Election Results Live Updates Maharashtra Election Results
Jharkhand Election Results
Bypoll Election Results
WhatsApp cofounder Jan Koumhad supported Lurie, while two former entrepreneurs, Bilal Mahmood and Danny Sauter, were also elected to the city’s board of supervisors.
Upon taking office on January 8, Lurie, who has no prior experience at City Hall, will face the challenge of resolving San Francisco's public safety crisis, a pressing issue that has driven numerous tech leaders to relocate.
Other tech executives embraced the chance to focus their industry's skills on San Francisco's problems. “I’m excited to help the city I love, and where OpenAI was started,” Altman said.
Meta loses German court fight over data leak
Meta Platforms risks being on the hook for compensation claims from thousands of German users after it lost a top court ruling over the 2021 global leak of half a billion people’s private data.

The Germany federal court eased the standard of proof for users seeking compensation, saying that it’s enough to show people were victims of the leak and that they don’t have to additionally prove they suffered.

Overturning a lower court ruling won by Meta, the judges also said that in situations involving mere loss of control of the data, compensation is likely to be about 100 euros.
The litigation is linked to a massive data leak, when personal information on 533 million Facebook users — including phone numbers and email addresses — emerged on a hacker website in 2021.
Social media giants face $32-million fines under Australia ban
Global social media firms could face fines of as much as a $50 million if they fail to enforce the Australian government’s new social media ban on children under the age of 16.
Under the bill, big tech will be required to take reasonable steps to prevent children from having an account, with fines to be levied on firms that breach the legislation.
There will be exceptions embedded into the legislation for certain types of online services, including messaging applications, online gaming platforms and health and education.
Though there are no details yet on how the ban will be enforced, new provisions would be included in the legislation to underpin the ban.
Google ’s Chrome worth up to $20 billion if judge orders sale
Alphabet’s Chrome browser could go for as much as $20 billion if a judge agrees to a US Justice Department proposal to sell the business , in what would be a historic crackdown on one of the world’s biggest tech companies.
The department will ask judge Amit Mehta, who had ruled in August that Google illegally monopolised the search market, to require measures related to AI and the Android smartphone operating system.
The proposals may potentially reshape the online search market and the burgeoning AI industry. Owning the world’s most popular web browser is key for Google’s ads business. Should a sale proceed, Chrome would be worth at least $15-$20 billion, given it has over three billion monthly active users.
